questions.question: Put the historical eras of cognitive neuroscience in chronological order (earliest first).
questions.explanation: The progression was: Natural Philosophy (Helmholtz, Wundt, James) → Phrenology (Gall) → Neuron Doctrine (Cajal) → Behaviourism (Skinner) → Cognitive Revolution (1950s-60s) → Modern Cognitive Neuroscience (fMRI era, 1990s+).

questions.question: Order the steps of neural signal transmission from start to finish.
items: Action potential travels down the axon
Neurotransmitter is released into the synapse
correct_order: Action potential travels down the axon
Neurotransmitter is released into the synapse
questions.explanation: Neural transmission follows this sequence: dendrites receive input → the soma integrates it → if threshold is reached, an action potential fires down the axon → neurotransmitter is released at the synapse → it binds to the next neuron's receptors.

questions.question: Order these contributors chronologically by when their major contributions occurred (earliest first).
questions.explanation: Helmholtz (mid 1800s) → Gall (early-mid 1800s, though overlapping) → Cajal (late 1800s-early 1900s) → Penfield (1930s-50s) → Chomsky (1950s-60s). Note: Gall and Helmholtz overlapped but Helmholtz's Natural Philosophy tradition is generally placed as the earlier framework.

questions.question: Order the development of ideas about brain organisation from simplest to most sophisticated.
items: Brain is made of discrete neurons
Neural correlates of cognition via imaging
Brain is a 'black box' — only study behaviour
correct_order: Brain is made of discrete neurons
Brain is a 'black box' — only study behaviour
Neural correlates of cognition via imaging
questions.explanation: Understanding progressed from localisation (phrenology) → cellular structure (neuron doctrine) → behaviourist detour → imaging-based correlates → modern multimethodological models of cognition grounded in neurobiology.

questions.question: Order the brain structures from most superficial (outer) to deepest (inner).
questions.explanation: The cerebral cortex is the outermost layer of the brain. Moving inward, the amygdala and hippocampus sit in the medial temporal lobe, and the thalamus is a deep diencephalic structure at the centre of the brain.

questions.question: Order the steps by which fMRI detects brain activity.
items: MRI scanner detects change in blood oxygenation
Increased blood flow to active region
correct_order: Increased blood flow to active region
MRI scanner detects change in blood oxygenation
questions.explanation: The fMRI chain: neurons fire → they use energy/oxygen → blood flow increases to replenish → the MRI detects changes in oxygenated vs deoxygenated blood → this is recorded as the BOLD signal.

questions.question: Order the progression from Gall's phrenology to modern neuroscience in terms of methodological rigour.
questions.explanation: Methodological rigour progressed from pseudoscientific skull reading → microscopic staining techniques → controlled behavioural experiments → direct cortical stimulation → modern genomics combined with neuroimaging.

questions.question: Order the key events of the Neuron Doctrine story.
items: Cajal demonstrates neurons are discrete cells
Golgi and Cajal share the Nobel Prize
Debate between reticular theory and neuron theory
correct_order: Cajal demonstrates neurons are discrete cells
Debate between reticular theory and neuron theory
Golgi and Cajal share the Nobel Prize
questions.explanation: Golgi invented the stain → Cajal used it to show discrete neurons → a fierce debate ensued (Golgi favoured reticular theory) → the neuron doctrine won → they shared the 1906 Nobel Prize despite disagreeing.

questions.question: Order these paradigm shifts in the study of the mind from earliest to latest.
items: Internal mental processes can be studied scientifically
Only observable behaviour should be studied
Mind can be studied through philosophical reflection
Brain biology constrains and enables cognition
correct_order: Mind can be studied through philosophical reflection
Only observable behaviour should be studied
Internal mental processes can be studied scientifically
Brain biology constrains and enables cognition
questions.explanation: The progression: philosophical reflection (Natural Philosophy) → behaviourist restriction to observables → cognitive revolution reopens mental processes → modern cognitive neuroscience grounds cognition in neurobiology.

questions.question: Order the levels of analysis in neuroscience from smallest to largest scale.
questions.explanation: Neuroscience spans scales from molecular (neurotransmitters) → synaptic (connections between neurons) → cellular (individual neurons) → systems (brain regions/networks) → whole-brain (imaging studies).
